entries.content: I'll start by thoroughly investigating the codebase and creating a comprehensive implementation plan for setting up the dependency injection framework. Let me begin with PHASE 0 - Deep Codebase Investigation.

entries.detail:
1→# Set up dependency injection framework
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→Integrate Hilt (or Koin) for dependency injection from the start. Structuring the app with DI from the beginning avoids costly refactoring later and makes the codebase inherently testable with clear separation of concerns.
     6→
     7→## Rationale
     8→
     9→Retrofitting dependency injection into an existing codebase is significantly harder than starting with it. This enables clean architecture patterns for the inventory management features.
    10→
    11→---
    12→*This spec was created from ideation and is pending detailed specification.*
    13→

1→# Inventory APK
     2→
     3→An Android application for efficient inventory management and tracking.
     4→
     5→## 📋 Overview
     6→
     7→Inventory APK is a mobile inventory management solution designed to help businesses and individuals track, manage, and organize their inventory items efficiently. Built natively for Android, the application provides a streamlined interface for managing stock levels, tracking item movements, and generating reports on the go.
     8→
     9→## 🎯 Purpose
    10→
    11→Traditional inventory management often requires desktop software or complex web applications. Inventory APK brings the power of inventory tracking directly to your mobile device, enabling:
    12→
    13→- **Real-time tracking**: Update inventory status immediately as items move
    14→- **Offline-first operation**: Manage inventory without constant internet connectivity
    15→- **Mobile convenience**: Use your smartphone to scan, update, and view inventory anywhere
    16→- **Simplified workflow**: Intuitive interface designed for quick data entry and retrieval
    17→
    18→## ✨ Key Features
    19→
    20→### Core Functionality (Planned)
    21→- **Item Management**: Add, edit, and delete inventory items with detailed information
    22→- **Barcode Scanning**: Quick item lookup and data entry using device camera
    23→- **Stock Tracking**: Monitor quantity levels with low-stock alerts
    24→- **Category Organization**: Group items by custom categories and tags
    25→- **Search & Filter**: Quickly find items using powerful search capabilities
    26→- **Offline Support**: Full functionality without internet connection
    27→- **Data Export**: Export inventory data to CSV/Excel formats
    28→
    29→### Advanced Features (Future Roadmap)
    30→- **Multi-location Support**: Manage inventory across multiple warehouses or stores
    31→- **Supplier Management**: Track vendor information and purchase orders
    32→- **Stock Movement History**: Complete audit trail of inventory changes
    33→- **Reporting & Analytics**: Visual insights into inventory trends
    34→- **Cloud Sync**: Synchronize data across multiple devices
    35→- **Multi-user Access**: Collaborative inventory management with role-based permissions
    36→
    37→> **Note**: This project is in active development. See [ROADMAP.md](ROADMAP.md) for detailed feature timeline and MVP scope.
    38→
    39→## 🚀 Getting Started
    40→
    41→### Prerequisites
    42→
    43→Before you begin, ensure you have the following installed:
    44→
    45→- **Android Studio**: Arctic Fox (2020.3.1) or later
    46→- **JDK**: Java Development Kit 11 or higher
    47→- **Android SDK**: API Level 24 (Android 7.0) minimum
    48→- **Git**: For version control
    49→
    50→### Installation
    51→
    52→1. **Clone the repository**
    53→   ```bash
    54→   git clone https://github.com/yourusername/inventory-apk.git
    55→   cd inventory-apk
    56→   ```
    57→
    58→2. **Open in Android Studio**
    59→   - Launch Android Studio
    60→   - Select "Open an Existing Project"
    61→   - Navigate to the cloned repository folder
    62→   - Click "OK"
    63→
    64→3. **Sync Gradle dependencies**
    65→   - Android Studio will automatically prompt to sync
    66→   - Or manually sync via: File → Sync Project with Gradle Files
    67→
    68→4. **Configure Android Emulator** (optional)
    69→   - Open AVD Manager (Tools → AVD Manager)
    70→   - Create a new Virtual Device with API Level 24+
    71→   - Or connect a physical Android device with USB debugging enabled
    72→
    73→5. **Build and Run**
    74→   - Click the "Run" button (green play icon)
    75→   - Select your target device (emulator or physical device)
    76→   - The app will build and install automatically
    77→
    78→### Quick Start Guide
    79→
    80→Once the app is installed:
    81→
    82→1. **First Launch**: The app will create a local database for your inventory
    83→2. **Add Your First Item**:
    84→   - Tap the "+" floating action button
    85→   - Enter item details (name, SKU, quantity, etc.)
    86→   - Optionally scan a barcode for quick entry
    87→   - Save the item
    88→3. **View Inventory**: Browse your items in the main list view
    89→4. **Update Stock**: Tap any item to adjust quantity or edit details
    90→5. **Search Items**: Use the search bar to filter by name, SKU, or category
    91→
    92→> **Note**: As this is a greenfield project, the actual implementation is in progress. Check the [development roadmap](ROADMAP.md) for current status.
    93→
    94→## 📚 Documentation
    95→
    96→- **[ROADMAP.md](ROADMAP.md)** - Development roadmap, MVP scope, and milestones
    97→- **[CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md)** - Guidelines for contributing to the project
    98→- **[docs/ARCHITECTURE.md](docs/ARCHITECTURE.md)** - Technical architecture and design decisions
    99→- **[docs/features/](docs/features/)** - Detailed feature specifications
   100→
   101→## 🏗️ Project Status
   102→
   103→**Current Phase**: Initial Planning & Documentation
   104→
   105→This is a greenfield project currently in the planning and specification phase. Core documentation is being established to guide development. Active development of features will begin once the MVP scope is finalized.
   106→
   107→See [ROADMAP.md](ROADMAP.md) for detailed development timeline and priorities.
   108→
   109→## 🛠️ Technology Stack
   110→
   111→**Target Platform**: Android 7.0 (API 24) and above
   112→
   113→**Planned Technologies**:
   114→- **Language**: Kotlin
   115→- **Architecture**: MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel)
   116→- **Database**: Room (SQLite)
   117→- **Dependency Injection**: Hilt/Dagger
   118→- **Barcode Scanning**: ML Kit or ZXing
   119→- **UI Framework**: Jetpack Compose / Material Design 3
   120→
   121→> See [docs/ARCHITECTURE.md](docs/ARCHITECTURE.md) for detailed technical decisions and rationale.
   122→
   123→## 🤝 Contributing
   124→
   125→We welcome contributions from the community! Whether you're fixing bugs, adding features, or improving documentation, your help is appreciated.
   126→
   127→Please read [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for:
   128→- Development setup instructions
   129→- Coding standards and conventions
   130→- Pull request process
   131→- Issue reporting guidelines
   132→
   133→## 📝 License
   134→
   135→This project's license is to be determined. Check back soon for licensing information.
   136→
   137→## 📧 Contact & Support
   138→
   139→- **Issues**: [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/yourusername/inventory-apk/issues)
   140→- **Discussions**: [GitHub Discussions](https://github.com/yourusername/inventory-apk/discussions)
   141→
   142→## 🙏 Acknowledgments
   143→
   144→This project is built with modern Android development best practices and leverages the Android Jetpack suite of libraries.
   145→
   146→---
   147→
   148→**Status**: 🚧 In Development | **Version**: 0.1.0-alpha | **Last Updated**: March 2026
   149→

entries.content:
Perfect! I've completed the planning phase for Task 004: Set up dependency injection framework. Here's a summary of what has been created:

## 📋 Planning Complete

### Files Created:

1. **project_index.json** - Defines the Android project structure, tech stack (Kotlin, Hilt, Gradle), and conventions
2. **context.json** - Documents Hilt setup requirements, patterns, and files to create
3. **implementation_plan.json** - Complete implementation plan with:
   - 6 phases
   - 11 subtasks
   - Sequential execution (1 worker recommended)
   - Low risk verification strategy
4. **init.sh** - Environment verification script (checks Java/Android SDK)
5. **build-progress.txt** - Progress tracking document

### Implementation Plan Overview:

**Workflow Type:** SIMPLE (greenfield setup, single service)

**Phases:**
1. **Gradle Project Setup** (3 subtasks) - Create root build files, app module config, AndroidManifest
2. **Hilt Integration** (2 subtasks) - Add Hilt dependencies and plugins to Gradle
3. **Application Setup** (1 subtask) - Create InventoryApplication class with @HiltAndroidApp
4. **DI Modules** (2 subtasks) - Create AppModule and DatabaseModule as examples
5. **Example Usage** (2 subtasks) - Create MainActivity and MainViewModel to demonstrate DI
6. **Documentation** (2 subtasks) - Create DI_SETUP.md and verify full build

**Key Characteristics:**
- Greenfield Android project (no existing code)
- Uses Kotlin with Hilt for dependency injection
- MVVM architecture pattern
- Target SDK: 34, Min SDK: 24
- Sequential execution required due to dependencies

**Verification:**
- Primary: Gradle build success (`./gradlew clean app:assembleDebug`)
- Risk Level: Low (no existing functionality to break)
- Build verification only (no unit tests required for setup task)

### Next Steps:

The **Coder Agent** will now take over to:
1. Implement Phase 1: Create the Gradle project structure
2. Continue through phases 2-6 sequentially
3. Verify each subtask with the specified commands
4. Commit code changes (NOT the spec files)

**To start implementation:**
```bash
source auto-claude/.venv/bin/activate
python auto-claude/run.py --spec 004 --parallel 1
```

---

**⚠️ IMPORTANT:** As instructed, I have NOT:
- Implemented any code
- Started any subtasks
- Committed the planning files (they are gitignored)
- Modified the main project (working in isolated worktree)

My role as Planner Agent is complete. The implementation will be handled by a separate Coder Agent session.
I'll help you implement subtask-1-1 to create the root Gradle configuration files. Let me start by reading the specification and understanding the requirements.

entries.detail:
1→# Set up dependency injection framework
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→Integrate Hilt (or Koin) for dependency injection from the start. Structuring the app with DI from the beginning avoids costly refactoring later and makes the codebase inherently testable with clear separation of concerns.
     6→
     7→## Rationale
     8→
     9→Retrofitting dependency injection into an existing codebase is significantly harder than starting with it. This enables clean architecture patterns for the inventory management features.

1→# Set up dependency injection framework
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→Integrate Hilt (or Koin) for dependency injection from the start. Structuring the app with DI from the beginning avoids costly refactoring later and makes the codebase inherently testable with clear separation of concerns.
     6→
     7→## Rationale
     8→
     9→Retrofitting dependency injection into an existing codebase is significantly harder than starting with it. This enables clean architecture patterns for the inventory management features.

entries.detail:
1→# Set up dependency injection framework
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→Integrate Hilt (or Koin) for dependency injection from the start. Structuring the app with DI from the beginning avoids costly refactoring later and makes the codebase inherently testable with clear separation of concerns.
     6→
     7→## Rationale
     8→
     9→Retrofitting dependency injection into an existing codebase is significantly harder than starting with it. This enables clean architecture patterns for the inventory management features.
    10→
    11→---
    12→*This spec was created from ideation and is pending detailed specification.*
    13→

entries.detail:
1→# Set up dependency injection framework
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→Integrate Hilt (or Koin) for dependency injection from the start. Structuring the app with DI from the beginning avoids costly refactoring later and makes the codebase inherently testable with clear separation of concerns.
     6→
     7→## Rationale
     8→
     9→Retrofitting dependency injection into an existing codebase is significantly harder than starting with it. This enables clean architecture patterns for the inventory management features.
    10→
    11→---
    12→*This spec was created from ideation and is pending detailed specification.*
    13→

entries.detail:
1→# Set up dependency injection framework
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→Integrate Hilt (or Koin) for dependency injection from the start. Structuring the app with DI from the beginning avoids costly refactoring later and makes the codebase inherently testable with clear separation of concerns.
     6→
     7→## Rationale
     8→
     9→Retrofitting dependency injection into an existing codebase is significantly harder than starting with it. This enables clean architecture patterns for the inventory management features.
    10→
    11→---
    12→*This spec was created from ideation and is pending detailed specification.*
    13→

entries.detail:
1→# Set up dependency injection framework
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→Integrate Hilt (or Koin) for dependency injection from the start. Structuring the app with DI from the beginning avoids costly refactoring later and makes the codebase inherently testable with clear separation of concerns.
     6→
     7→## Rationale
     8→
     9→Retrofitting dependency injection into an existing codebase is significantly harder than starting with it. This enables clean architecture patterns for the inventory management features.
    10→

entries.content:
## ✅ Subtask Completed Successfully!

I've successfully completed **subtask-3-1**: Create InventoryApplication class with @HiltAndroidApp

### What Was Implemented:

1. **Created `InventoryApplication.kt`**
   - Located at: `app/src/main/java/com/inventory/app/InventoryApplication.kt`
   - Extends `Application` class
   - Annotated with `@HiltAndroidApp` to initialize Hilt dependency injection
   - Includes proper documentation

2. **Updated `AndroidManifest.xml`**
   - Added `android:name=".InventoryApplication"` to the `<application>` tag
   - This ensures the custom Application class is used when the app starts

### Implementation Details:

```kotlin
@HiltAndroidApp
class InventoryApplication : Application() {
    override fun onCreate() {
        super.onCreate()
        // Application initialization code goes here
    }
}
```

The manifest now correctly references this class, enabling Hilt to generate the necessary dependency injection code at compile time.

### Verification:

- ✅ Code is syntactically correct and follows Hilt conventions
- ✅ Files properly structured with correct package names
- ✅ @HiltAndroidApp annotation properly applied
- ✅ Manifest correctly references the Application class
- ✅ Changes committed to git (commit: 23df143)
- ✅ Implementation plan updated

### Note:

Full build verification with `./gradlew app:compileDebugKotlin` requires Android SDK installation, which is not available in this environment. This is consistent with previous subtasks. The implementation follows standard Hilt patterns and is ready for the next phase of DI module setup.
I'll help you implement subtask-4-1 to create the AppModule for application-level dependencies. Let me start by understanding the context and patterns.

entries.detail:
1→# Set up dependency injection framework
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→Integrate Hilt (or Koin) for dependency injection from the start. Structuring the app with DI from the beginning avoids costly refactoring later and makes the codebase inherently testable with clear separation of concerns.
     6→
     7→## Rationale
     8→
     9→Retrofitting dependency injection into an existing codebase is significantly harder than starting with it. This enables clean architecture patterns for the inventory management features.
    10→
    11→---
    12→*This spec was created from ideation and is pending detailed specification.*
    13→

entries.detail:
1→# Set up dependency injection framework
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→Integrate Hilt (or Koin) for dependency injection from the start. Structuring the app with DI from the beginning avoids costly refactoring later and makes the codebase inherently testable with clear separation of concerns.
     6→
     7→## Rationale
     8→
     9→Retrofitting dependency injection into an existing codebase is significantly harder than starting with it. This enables clean architecture patterns for the inventory management features.
    10→
    11→---
    12→*This spec was created from ideation and is pending detailed specification.*
    13→

1→package com.inventory.app.di
     2→
     3→import android.app.Application
     4→import android.content.Context
     5→import dagger.Module
     6→import dagger.Provides
     7→import dagger.hilt.InstallIn
     8→import dagger.hilt.components.SingletonComponent
     9→import javax.inject.Qualifier
    10→import javax.inject.Singleton
    11→
    12→/**
    13→ * Hilt module for application-level dependencies.
    14→ * Provides singleton instances of core application components.
    15→ */
    16→@Module
    17→@InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class)
    18→object AppModule {
    19→
    20→    /**
    21→     * Provides the application context.
    22→     * This can be injected anywhere in the app where Context is needed.
    23→     */
    24→    @Provides
    25→    @Singleton
    26→    fun provideApplicationContext(application: Application): Context {
    27→        return application.applicationContext
    28→    }
    29→
    30→    /**
    31→     * Provides the application name as a string.
    32→     * Qualified with @AppName to distinguish from other String dependencies.
    33→     */
    34→    @Provides
    35→    @Singleton
    36→    @AppName
    37→    fun provideAppName(context: Context): String {
    38→        return context.applicationInfo.loadLabel(context.packageManager).toString()
    39→    }
    40→
    41→    /**
    42→     * Provides the application package name.
    43→     * Qualified with @PackageName to distinguish from other String dependencies.
    44→     */
    45→    @Provides
    46→    @Singleton
    47→    @PackageName
    48→    fun providePackageName(context: Context): String {
    49→        return context.packageName
    50→    }
    51→}
    52→
    53→/**
    54→ * Qualifier annotation for the application name.
    55→ */
    56→@Qualifier
    57→@Retention(AnnotationRetention.BINARY)
    58→annotation class AppName
    59→
    60→/**
    61→ * Qualifier annotation for the package name.
    62→ */
    63→@Qualifier
    64→@Retention(AnnotationRetention.BINARY)
    65→annotation class PackageName

1→package com.inventory.app.di
     2→
     3→import android.app.Application
     4→import android.content.Context
     5→import dagger.Module
     6→import dagger.Provides
     7→import dagger.hilt.InstallIn
     8→import dagger.hilt.components.SingletonComponent
     9→import javax.inject.Qualifier
    10→import javax.inject.Singleton
    11→
    12→/**
    13→ * Hilt module for application-level dependencies.
    14→ * Provides singleton instances of core application components.
    15→ */
    16→@Module
    17→@InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class)
    18→object AppModule {
    19→
    20→    /**
    21→     * Provides the application context.
    22→     * This can be injected anywhere in the app where Context is needed.
    23→     */
    24→    @Provides
    25→    @Singleton
    26→    fun provideApplicationContext(application: Application): Context {
    27→        return application.applicationContext
    28→    }
    29→
    30→    /**
    31→     * Provides the application name as a string.
    32→     * Qualified with @AppName to distinguish from other String dependencies.
    33→     */
    34→    @Provides
    35→    @Singleton
    36→    @AppName
    37→    fun provideAppName(context: Context): String {
    38→        return context.applicationInfo.loadLabel(context.packageManager).toString()
    39→    }
    40→
    41→    /**
    42→     * Provides the application package name.
    43→     * Qualified with @PackageName to distinguish from other String dependencies.
    44→     */
    45→    @Provides
    46→    @Singleton
    47→    @PackageName
    48→    fun providePackageName(context: Context): String {
    49→        return context.packageName
    50→    }
    51→}
    52→
    53→/**
    54→ * Qualifier annotation for the application name.
    55→ */
    56→@Qualifier
    57→@Retention(AnnotationRetention.BINARY)
    58→annotation class AppName
    59→
    60→/**
    61→ * Qualifier annotation for the package name.
    62→ */
    63→@Qualifier
    64→@Retention(AnnotationRetention.BINARY)
    65→annotation class PackageName
    66→

    11→ * Demonstrates Hilt dependency injection with constructor injection.
    12→ * Injects app-level dependencies from AppModule to verify DI is working correctly.
    13→ */
    14→@HiltViewModel
    15→class MainViewModel @Inject constructor(
    16→    @AppName private val appName: String,
    17→    @PackageName private val packageName: String
    18→) : ViewModel() {
    19→
    20→    /**
    21→     * Returns the application name provided by Hilt DI.
    22→     */
    23→    fun getAppName(): String = appName
    24→
    25→    /**
    26→     * Returns the package name provided by Hilt DI.
    27→     */
    28→    fun getPackageName(): String = packageName
    29→

    31→     * Returns a greeting message with injected dependencies.
    32→     * Useful for demonstrating that dependency injection is working.
    33→     */
    34→    fun getGreetingMessage(): String {
    35→        return "Welcome to $appName!\nPackage: $packageName"
    36→    }
    37→}

entries.detail:
1→# Dependency Injection Setup Guide
     2→
     3→This document explains how Hilt dependency injection is configured in the Inventory Management app, how to create new modules, how to inject dependencies, and common patterns and best practices.
     4→
     5→## Table of Contents
     6→
     7→1. [Overview](#overview)
     8→2. [How Hilt is Configured](#how-hilt-is-configured)
     9→3. [Creating New Modules](#creating-new-modules)
    10→4. [Injecting Dependencies](#injecting-dependencies)
    11→5. [Common Patterns and Best Practices](#common-patterns-and-best-practices)
    12→6. [Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting)
    13→
    14→---
    15→
    16→## Overview
    17→
    18→This project uses [Hilt](https://dagger.dev/hilt/) for dependency injection. Hilt is a dependency injection library for Android that reduces boilerplate code and provides compile-time correctness, performance, and Android-specific integration.
    19→
    20→**Key Benefits:**
    21→- Simplified dependency management
    22→- Compile-time validation of dependencies
    23→- Automatic lifecycle management for Android components
    24→- Standardized way to provide and inject dependencies
    25→
    26→---
    27→
    28→## How Hilt is Configured
    29→
    30→### 1. Application Class
    31→
    32→The `InventoryApplication` class is annotated with `@HiltAndroidApp`, which triggers Hilt's code generation:
    33→
    34→```kotlin
    35→@HiltAndroidApp
    36→class InventoryApplication : Application() {
    37→    override fun onCreate() {
    38→        super.onCreate()
    39→        // Application initialization code goes here
    40→    }
    41→}
    42→```
    43→
    44→**Location:** `app/src/main/java/com/inventory/app/InventoryApplication.kt`
    45→
    46→**Important:** The application class must be registered in `AndroidManifest.xml`:
    47→```xml
    48→<application
    49→    android:name=".InventoryApplication"
    50→    ...>

   - Application class setup with `@HiltAndroidApp`
   - Existing modules (AppModule, DatabaseModule)
   - Gradle configuration

2. **How to Create New Modules**
   - Module templates and structure
   - Component scopes (Singleton, Activity, Fragment, ViewModel, Service)
   - Examples for network modules, qualifiers, and interface bindings

3. **How to Inject Dependencies**
   - Constructor injection (recommended approach)
   - Field injection for Android framework classes
   - Injection in ViewModels, Activities, Fragments, and Compose
   - Context injection patterns

4. **Common Patterns and Best Practices**
   - Module organization by feature/layer
   - Proper scoping strategies
   - Interface-based design for testability
   - Qualifier usage for same-type dependencies
   - Testing with Hilt
   - Asynchronous initialization
   - Documentation standards

5. **Troubleshooting Guide**
   - Common errors and solutions
   - Debugging tips
   - Additional resources

6. **Project-Specific Examples**
   - Current module structure
   - Usage examples from the existing codebase

1→# Set up dependency injection framework
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→Integrate Hilt (or Koin) for dependency injection from the start. Structuring the app with DI from the beginning avoids costly refactoring later and makes the codebase inherently testable with clear separation of concerns.
     6→
     7→## Rationale
     8→
     9→Retrofitting dependency injection into an existing codebase is significantly harder than starting with it. This enables clean architecture patterns for the inventory management features.
    10→

1→package com.inventory.app.di
     2→
     3→import android.app.Application
     4→import android.content.Context
     5→import dagger.Module
     6→import dagger.Provides
     7→import dagger.hilt.InstallIn
     8→import dagger.hilt.components.SingletonComponent
     9→import javax.inject.Qualifier
    10→import javax.inject.Singleton
    11→
    12→/**
    13→ * Hilt module for application-level dependencies.
    14→ * Provides singleton instances of core application components.
    15→ */
    16→@Module
    17→@InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class)
    18→object AppModule {
    19→
    20→    /**
    21→     * Provides the application context.
    22→     * This can be injected anywhere in the app where Context is needed.
    23→     */
    24→    @Provides
    25→    @Singleton
    26→    fun provideApplicationContext(application: Application): Context {
    27→        return application.applicationContext
    28→    }
    29→
    30→    /**
    31→     * Provides the application name as a string.
    32→     * Qualified with @AppName to distinguish from other String dependencies.
    33→     */
    34→    @Provides
    35→    @Singleton
    36→    @AppName
    37→    fun provideAppName(context: Context): String {
    38→        return context.applicationInfo.loadLabel(context.packageManager).toString()
    39→    }
    40→
    41→    /**
    42→     * Provides the application package name.
    43→     * Qualified with @PackageName to distinguish from other String dependencies.
    44→     */
    45→    @Provides
    46→    @Singleton
    47→    @PackageName
    48→    fun providePackageName(context: Context): String {
    49→        return context.packageName
    50→    }
    51→}
    52→
    53→/**
    54→ * Qualifier annotation for the application name.
    55→ */
    56→@Qualifier
    57→@Retention(AnnotationRetention.BINARY)
    58→annotation class AppName
    59→
    60→/**
    61→ * Qualifier annotation for the package name.
    62→ */
    63→@Qualifier
    64→@Retention(AnnotationRetention.BINARY)
    65→annotation class PackageName
    66→

1→# Set up dependency injection framework
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→Integrate Hilt (or Koin) for dependency injection from the start. Structuring the app with DI from the beginning avoids costly refactoring later and makes the codebase inherently testable with clear separation of concerns.
     6→
     7→## Rationale
     8→
     9→Retrofitting dependency injection into an existing codebase is significantly harder than starting with it. This enables clean architecture patterns for the inventory management features.

1→package com.inventory.app.di
     2→
     3→import android.app.Application
     4→import android.content.Context
     5→import dagger.Module
     6→import dagger.Provides
     7→import dagger.hilt.InstallIn
     8→import dagger.hilt.components.SingletonComponent
     9→import javax.inject.Qualifier
    10→import javax.inject.Singleton
    11→
    12→/**
    13→ * Hilt module for application-level dependencies.
    14→ * Provides singleton instances of core application components.
    15→ */
    16→@Module
    17→@InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class)
    18→object AppModule {
    19→
    20→    /**
    21→     * Provides the application context.
    22→     * This can be injected anywhere in the app where Context is needed.
    23→     */
    24→    @Provides
    25→    @Singleton
    26→    fun provideApplicationContext(application: Application): Context {
    27→        return application.applicationContext
    28→    }
    29→
    30→    /**
    31→     * Provides the application name as a string.
    32→     * Qualified with @AppName to distinguish from other String dependencies.
    33→     */
    34→    @Provides
    35→    @Singleton
    36→    @AppName
    37→    fun provideAppName(context: Context): String {
    38→        return context.applicationInfo.loadLabel(context.packageManager).toString()
    39→    }
    40→
    41→    /**
    42→     * Provides the application package name.
    43→     * Qualified with @PackageName to distinguish from other String dependencies.
    44→     */
    45→    @Provides
    46→    @Singleton
    47→    @PackageName
    48→    fun providePackageName(context: Context): String {
    49→        return context.packageName
    50→    }
    51→}
    52→
    53→/**
    54→ * Qualifier annotation for the application name.
    55→ */
    56→@Qualifier
    57→@Retention(AnnotationRetention.BINARY)
    58→annotation class AppName
    59→
    60→/**
    61→ * Qualifier annotation for the package name.
    62→ */
    63→@Qualifier
    64→@Retention(AnnotationRetention.BINARY)
    65→annotation class PackageName
    66→

1→# Dependency Injection Setup Guide
     2→
     3→This document explains how Hilt dependency injection is configured in the Inventory Management app, how to create new modules, how to inject dependencies, and common patterns and best practices.
     4→
     5→## Table of Contents
     6→
     7→1. [Overview](#overview)
     8→2. [How Hilt is Configured](#how-hilt-is-configured)
     9→3. [Creating New Modules](#creating-new-modules)
    10→4. [Injecting Dependencies](#injecting-dependencies)
    11→5. [Common Patterns and Best Practices](#common-patterns-and-best-practices)
    12→6. [Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting)
    13→
    14→---
    15→
    16→## Overview
    17→
    18→This project uses [Hilt](https://dagger.dev/hilt/) for dependency injection. Hilt is a dependency injection library for Android that reduces boilerplate code and provides compile-time correctness, performance, and Android-specific integration.
    19→
    20→**Key Benefits:**
    21→- Simplified dependency management
    22→- Compile-time validation of dependencies
    23→- Automatic lifecycle management for Android components
    24→- Standardized way to provide and inject dependencies
    25→
    26→---
    27→
    28→## How Hilt is Configured
    29→
    30→### 1. Application Class
    31→
    32→The `InventoryApplication` class is annotated with `@HiltAndroidApp`, which triggers Hilt's code generation:
    33→
    34→```kotlin
    35→@HiltAndroidApp
    36→class InventoryApplication : Application() {
    37→    override fun onCreate() {
    38→        super.onCreate()
    39→        // Application initialization code goes here
    40→    }
    41→}
    42→```
    43→
    44→**Location:** `app/src/main/java/com/inventory/app/InventoryApplication.kt`
    45→
    46→**Important:** The application class must be registered in `AndroidManifest.xml`:
    47→```xml
    48→<application
    49→    android:name=".InventoryApplication"
    50→    ...>
    51→```
    52→
    53→### 2. Existing Modules
    54→
    55→The project includes two Hilt modules:
    56→
    57→#### AppModule
    58→**Location:** `app/src/main/java/com/inventory/app/di/AppModule.kt`
    59→
    60→Provides application-level dependencies:
    61→- Application Context
    62→- App Name (qualified with `@AppName`)
    63→- Package Name (qualified with `@PackageName`)
    64→
    65→#### DatabaseModule
    66→**Location:** `app/src/main/java/com/inventory/app/di/DatabaseModule.kt`
    67→
    68→Provides database-related configuration:
    69→- Database Name (qualified with `@DatabaseName`)
    70→- Database Version (qualified with `@DatabaseVersion`)
    71→
    72→**Note:** Room database integration is a placeholder and will be added in future tasks.
    73→
    74→### 3. Gradle Dependencies
    75→
    76→Hilt dependencies are configured in `app/build.gradle.kts`:
    77→
    78→```kotlin
    79→plugins {
    80→    id("com.google.dagger.hilt.android")
    81→    kotlin("kapt")
    82→}
    83→
    84→dependencies {
    85→    // Hilt
    86→    implementation("com.google.dagger:hilt-android:2.48")
    87→    kapt("com.google.dagger:hilt-compiler:2.48")
    88→}
    89→```
    90→
    91→---
    92→
    93→## Creating New Modules
    94→
    95→### Module Structure
    96→
    97→A Hilt module is a class annotated with `@Module` and `@InstallIn` that tells Hilt how to provide instances of certain types.
    98→
    99→### Basic Module Template
   100→
   101→```kotlin
   102→package com.inventory.app.di
   103→
   104→import dagger.Module
   105→import dagger.Provides
   106→import dagger.hilt.InstallIn
   107→import dagger.hilt.components.SingletonComponent
   108→import javax.inject.Singleton
   109→
   110→@Module
   111→@InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class)
   112→object YourModule {
   113→
   114→    @Provides
   115→    @Singleton
   116→    fun provideYourDependency(): YourType {
   117→        return YourType()
   118→    }
   119→}
   120→```
   121→
   122→### Component Scopes
   123→
   124→The `@InstallIn` annotation specifies which Android component the module should be installed in:
   125→
   126→| Component | Lifecycle | Use Case |
   127→|-----------|-----------|----------|
   128→| `SingletonComponent` | Application lifetime | App-wide singletons (databases, API clients) |
   129→| `ActivityComponent` | Activity lifetime | Activity-specific dependencies |
   130→| `FragmentComponent` | Fragment lifetime | Fragment-specific dependencies |
   131→| `ViewModelComponent` | ViewModel lifetime | ViewModel-specific dependencies |
   132→| `ServiceComponent` | Service lifetime | Service-specific dependencies |
   133→
   134→### Example: Network Module
   135→
   136→```kotlin
   137→@Module
   138→@InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class)
   139→object NetworkModule {
   140→
   141→    @Provides
   142→    @Singleton
   143→    fun provideOkHttpClient(): OkHttpClient {
   144→        return OkHttpClient.Builder()
   145→            .connectTimeout(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS)
   146→            .readTimeout(30, TimeUnit.SECONDS)
   147→            .build()
   148→    }
   149→
   150→    @Provides
   151→    @Singleton
   152→    fun provideRetrofit(okHttpClient: OkHttpClient): Retrofit {
   153→        return Retrofit.Builder()
   154→            .baseUrl("https://api.example.com/")
   155→            .client(okHttpClient)
   156→            .addConverterFactory(GsonConverterFactory.create())
   157→            .build()
   158→    }
   159→
   160→    @Provides
   161→    @Singleton
   162→    fun provideApiService(retrofit: Retrofit): ApiService {
   163→        return retrofit.create(ApiService::class.java)
   164→    }
   165→}
   166→```
   167→
   168→### Using Qualifiers
   169→
   170→When you need to provide multiple instances of the same type, use qualifiers to distinguish them:
   171→
   172→```kotlin
   173→// Define qualifiers
   174→@Qualifier
   175→@Retention(AnnotationRetention.BINARY)
   176→annotation class ApiBaseUrl
   177→
   178→@Qualifier
   179→@Retention(AnnotationRetention.BINARY)
   180→annotation class ImageBaseUrl
   181→
   182→// Use in module
   183→@Module
   184→@InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class)
   185→object UrlModule {
   186→
   187→    @Provides
   188→    @ApiBaseUrl
   189→    fun provideApiBaseUrl(): String = "https://api.example.com/"
   190→
   191→    @Provides
   192→    @ImageBaseUrl
   193→    fun provideImageBaseUrl(): String = "https://images.example.com/"
   194→}
   195→```
   196→
   197→### Binding Interfaces
   198→
   199→When you need to bind an interface to its implementation:
   200→
   201→```kotlin
   202→@Module
   203→@InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class)
   204→abstract class RepositoryModule {
   205→
   206→    @Binds
   207→    @Singleton
   208→    abstract fun bindInventoryRepository(
   209→        impl: InventoryRepositoryImpl
   210→    ): InventoryRepository
   211→}
   212→```
   213→
   214→**Note:** When using `@Binds`, the module must be an abstract class, and the method must be abstract.
   215→
   216→---
   217→
   218→## Injecting Dependencies
   219→
   220→### 1. Constructor Injection (Recommended)
   221→
   222→The preferred way to inject dependencies. Works with classes you own.
   223→
   224→#### In ViewModels
   225→
   226→```kotlin
   227→@HiltViewModel
   228→class MainViewModel @Inject constructor(
   229→    @AppName private val appName: String,
   230→    @PackageName private val packageName: String
   231→) : ViewModel() {
   232→
   233→    fun getAppName(): String = appName
   234→
   235→    fun getPackageName(): String = packageName
   236→}
   237→```
   238→
   239→**Key Points:**
   240→- Annotate the ViewModel with `@HiltViewModel`
   241→- Use `@Inject` on the constructor
   242→- Use qualifiers when needed (e.g., `@AppName`)
   243→
   244→#### In Regular Classes
   245→
   246→```kotlin
   247→class InventoryRepository @Inject constructor(
   248→    private val database: InventoryDatabase,
   249→    private val apiService: ApiService
   250→) {
   251→    // Repository implementation
   252→}
   253→```
   254→
   255→### 2. Field Injection
   256→
   257→Use when constructor injection isn't possible (e.g., Android framework classes).
   258→
   259→#### In Activities
   260→
   261→```kotlin
   262→@AndroidEntryPoint
   263→class MainActivity : ComponentActivity() {
   264→
   265→    @Inject
   266→    lateinit var repository: InventoryRepository
   267→
   268→    override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) {
   269→        super.onCreate(savedInstanceState)
   270→        // repository is now available
   271→    }
   272→}
   273→```
   274→
   275→**Important:** Always annotate the Activity/Fragment with `@AndroidEntryPoint`.
   276→
   277→#### In Fragments
   278→
   279→```kotlin
   280→@AndroidEntryPoint
   281→class InventoryFragment : Fragment() {
   282→
   283→    @Inject
   284→    lateinit var viewModelFactory: ViewModelProvider.Factory
   285→
   286→    private val viewModel: InventoryViewModel by viewModels()
   287→}
   288→```
   289→
   290→#### In Compose
   291→
   292→For Compose, use `hiltViewModel()`:
   293→
   294→```kotlin
   295→@Composable
   296→fun InventoryScreen(
   297→    viewModel: InventoryViewModel = hiltViewModel()
   298→) {
   299→    // Use viewModel
   300→}
   301→```
   302→
   303→### 3. Injecting Context
   304→
   305→Context is automatically provided by Hilt:
   306→
   307→```kotlin
   308→// Application Context (singleton scope)
   309→@Inject constructor(
   310→    @ApplicationContext context: Context
   311→)
   312→
   313→// Activity Context (activity scope)
   314→@Inject constructor(
   315→    @ActivityContext context: Context
   316→)
   317→```
   318→
   319→**Example from AppModule:**
   320→```kotlin
   321→@Provides
   322→@Singleton
   323→fun provideApplicationContext(application: Application): Context {
   324→    return application.applicationContext
   325→}
   326→```
   327→
   328→---
   329→
   330→## Common Patterns and Best Practices
   331→
   332→### 1. Module Organization
   333→
   334→Organize modules by feature or layer:
   335→
   336→```
   337→di/
   338→├── AppModule.kt           # Applicat

... [truncated - full output was 20547 chars]

1→# Set up dependency injection framework
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→Integrate Hilt (or Koin) for dependency injection from the start. Structuring the app with DI from the beginning avoids costly refactoring later and makes the codebase inherently testable with clear separation of concerns.
     6→
     7→## Rationale
     8→
     9→Retrofitting dependency injection into an existing codebase is significantly harder than starting with it. This enables clean architecture patterns for the inventory management features.

1→package com.inventory.app.di
     2→
     3→import android.app.Application
     4→import android.content.Context
     5→import dagger.Module
     6→import dagger.Provides
     7→import dagger.hilt.InstallIn
     8→import dagger.hilt.components.SingletonComponent
     9→import javax.inject.Qualifier
    10→import javax.inject.Singleton
    11→
    12→/**
    13→ * Hilt module for application-level dependencies.
    14→ * Provides singleton instances of core application components.
    15→ */
    16→@Module
    17→@InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class)
    18→object AppModule {
    19→
    20→    /**
    21→     * Provides the application context.
    22→     * This can be injected anywhere in the app where Context is needed.
    23→     */
    24→    @Provides
    25→    @Singleton
    26→    fun provideApplicationContext(application: Application): Context {
    27→        return application.applicationContext
    28→    }
    29→
    30→    /**
    31→     * Provides the application name as a string.
    32→     * Qualified with @AppName to distinguish from other String dependencies.
    33→     */
    34→    @Provides
    35→    @Singleton
    36→    @AppName
    37→    fun provideAppName(context: Context): String {
    38→        return context.applicationInfo.loadLabel(context.packageManager).toString()
    39→    }
    40→
    41→    /**
    42→     * Provides the application package name.
    43→     * Qualified with @PackageName to distinguish from other String dependencies.
    44→     */
    45→    @Provides
    46→    @Singleton
    47→    @PackageName
    48→    fun providePackageName(context: Context): String {
    49→        return context.packageName
    50→    }
    51→}
    52→
    53→/**
    54→ * Qualifier annotation for the application name.
    55→ */
    56→@Qualifier
    57→@Retention(AnnotationRetention.BINARY)
    58→annotation class AppName
    59→
    60→/**
    61→ * Qualifier annotation for the package name.
    62→ */
    63→@Qualifier
    64→@Retention(AnnotationRetention.BINARY)
    65→annotation class PackageName
    66→

1→# Dependency Injection Setup Guide
     2→
     3→This document explains how Hilt dependency injection is configured in the Inventory Management app, how to create new modules, how to inject dependencies, and common patterns and best practices.
     4→
     5→## Table of Contents
     6→
     7→1. [Overview](#overview)
     8→2. [How Hilt is Configured](#how-hilt-is-configured)
     9→3. [Creating New Modules](#creating-new-modules)
    10→4. [Injecting Dependencies](#injecting-dependencies)
    11→5. [Common Patterns and Best Practices](#common-patterns-and-best-practices)
    12→6. [Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting)
    13→
    14→---
    15→
    16→## Overview
    17→
    18→This project uses [Hilt](https://dagger.dev/hilt/) for dependency injection. Hilt is a dependency injection library for Android that reduces boilerplate code and provides compile-time correctness, performance, and Android-specific integration.
    19→
    20→**Key Benefits:**
    21→- Simplified dependency management
    22→- Compile-time validation of dependencies
    23→- Automatic lifecycle management for Android components
    24→- Standardized way to provide and inject dependencies
    25→
    26→---
    27→
    28→## How Hilt is Configured
    29→
    30→### 1. Application Class
    31→
    32→The `InventoryApplication` class is annotated with `@HiltAndroidApp`, which triggers Hilt's code generation:
    33→
    34→```kotlin
    35→@HiltAndroidApp
    36→class InventoryApplication : Application() {
    37→    override fun onCreate() {
    38→        super.onCreate()
    39→        // Application initialization code goes here
    40→    }
    41→}
    42→```
    43→
    44→**Location:** `app/src/main/java/com/inventory/app/InventoryApplication.kt`
    45→
    46→**Important:** The application class must be registered in `AndroidManifest.xml`:
    47→```xml
    48→<application
    49→    android:name=".InventoryApplication"
    50→    ...>
    51→```
    52→
    53→### 2. Existing Modules
    54→
    55→The project includes two Hilt modules:
    56→
    57→#### AppModule
    58→**Location:** `app/src/main/java/com/inventory/app/di/AppModule.kt`
    59→
    60→Provides application-level dependencies:
    61→- Application Context
    62→- App Name (qualified with `@AppName`)
    63→- Package Name (qualified with `@PackageName`)
    64→
    65→#### DatabaseModule
    66→**Location:** `app/src/main/java/com/inventory/app/di/DatabaseModule.kt`
    67→
    68→Provides database-related configuration:
    69→- Database Name (qualified with `@DatabaseName`)
    70→- Database Version (qualified with `@DatabaseVersion`)
    71→
    72→**Note:** Room database integration is a placeholder and will be added in future tasks.
    73→
    74→### 3. Gradle Dependencies
    75→
    76→Hilt dependencies are configured in `app/build.gradle.kts`:
    77→
    78→```kotlin
    79→plugins {
    80→    id("com.google.dagger.hilt.android")
    81→    kotlin("kapt")
    82→}
    83→
    84→dependencies {
    85→    // Hilt
    86→    implementation("com.google.dagger:hilt-android:2.48")
    87→    kapt("com.google.dagger:hilt-compiler:2.48")
    88→}
    89→```
    90→
    91→---
    92→
    93→## Creating New Modules
    94→
    95→### Module Structure
    96→
    97→A Hilt module is a class annotated with `@Module` and `@InstallIn` that tells Hilt how to provide instances of certain types.
    98→
    99→### Basic Module Template
   100→
